speaker: interviewer
text: Victor Ungar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at-- in 1944-- actually, you were born in '24. This is '44. So you were about 20.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 20. Yeah. 19, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point you went to visit your-- to stay with your aunt. In which city, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Humenne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was technically in Slovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slovakia, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed. They took me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself? Did your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself. And I stayed with them for a short while. And when the fighting intensified on the Slovakian-Russian border, the Slovakian authorities were afraid that the, that the Jewish residents might side with the Russians. They, they evacuated all the Jewish people from Eastern Slovakia to Western Slovakia. And they assigned certain cities where they could go. So my aunt and my uncle, who lived in Medzilaborce, were, were ordered to go to villages and small towns near the capital, near Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about how you felt when you left your family. What, what were your greatest concerns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was worried about my parents. That was the first time that I had to make decisions for myself. Up to that point in time, there was no such thing that I had to do for myself. I'd never worked for anyone. I never earned a penny in my life. And most of it, I never had to make any decisions for myself. That point in time, I was told by my aunt and my cousin in Humenne and my uncle that I had to go to Bratislava, and I have to seek out my paternal relatives and tell them, here I am. Help me, you know, whatever you can. So they put me on a train. They gave me some false identities.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, they did not require any identity IDs with a picture. Just a birth certificate was sufficient, or, or a local-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or any written material that you were Slovakian. I spoke Slovakian. Since my-- since, up until 1938, I attended Slovakian schools. It wasn't very good, my Slovakian. But it was good enough for, for their, for the authorities, for the police, who were sort of not very literate, you know? It was OK for them. They couldn't detect that I might be not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, if everyone-- no one thought of me that I looked Jewish. Honest. I was dressed like a Jew. But otherwise, if the attire wasn't Jewish, then they-- I could pass for a non-Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I arrived in the capital city of Bratislava, after they were ordered to, to evacuate, I just had a little satchel with maybe a shirt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] underwear, and socks. I, I was approached by a uniformed person. And I thought, well, this is my end.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing he wanted to see, whether I didn't bring any, any food that was, that wasn't allowed to be brought into the city, like contraband. Because at that point in time, they were rationing. So he was just concerned about that. Well, I felt relieved. And I proceeded to the residence of my father's uncle. I came. I knocked on his door. And I told him who I am. They were friendly. They took me in because there was no other place to go. I had money on me. And right away, I went to the Jewish community. I lived in the Jewish area there. There was a street named Jewish-- the street of Jews. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for the time being, I didn't have a lot to do. I just hung around the Jewish community and-- with other Jews-- Hungarian Jews and Polish Jews and Slovakian Jews. We were just trying to see what we can do. Finally, we, we were told-- and we were helped-- by some other Jews to get some identity papers. I got myself a, a birth certificate in a name, by the name of Jan Suroviak. I picked that name out of, out of the-- not a telephone book-- but out of the advertisements that the local paper ran. And I get, I got a birth certificate. I guess that was sufficient.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made that birth certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the Jewish people who, I guess, who paid up, paid off someone who, who stole some of the forms, printed forms. And we made-- there was a fellow who was sort of an artist who copied the seal with a rubber-- it was a, it was a seal of Slovakia. They copied it on a-- they lifted it, actually, with a gelatin, a gelatin-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was-- they, they knew-- they had a trick. They lifted it, and then they applied it against-- they got a negative print. And they put it on the picture and, and on the seal. So that was-- so I had something to identify myself in case they stopped me. Later on, my-- after things got worse in Slovakia, they-- my parents decided to send my younger brother, who is still alive and lives close by, to Slovakia. After crossing the border-- there was a Jewish person who still lived under the Slovakia, protection of the Slovakian police, whom he paid up. And he was the-- he actually paid off, paid the police to, not to, not to bother the Jewish people who come, came across the border. And his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. Supposedly, he was also an informer. Some people claimed he was an informer.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Slovakians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was he informing on and to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Jews. Certain Jews. Certain Jews, he was doing a favor. Certain Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was this [NON-ENGLISH]-- this was the policeman who turned his back or the Jew who helped the other Jews as they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] was a Jewish person. And he was, some people said he was an informer. So after he, my brother came across the border, he came with several other people. And they told him that my father was a wealthy person. And he demanded sort of a ransom from me. I said, for heaven's sake, I don't have a penny, you know. I had $100 that my father gave me that I, that I smuggled in. But you know, I knew that we need it, you know. I'll need it for my brother and myself. I said, I don't have no money. I cried, you know. So then he let him go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did this all take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bratislava. He came to Bratislava and told me that I had my brother. He's holding him and some other people. And that I have money and I should-- if I don't have no money, I should raise money. And he wanted several hundred dollars from me. I said, I don't have any money. So then he released him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the other people as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wasn't concerned about the other people, you know. I was-- he was only trying to ransom, hold ransom my brother, as far as I was concerned. And I was pretty young, you know. I wasn't even 20. And like I told you, it was the first time in my life I had to deal with something like that. I was-- I myself didn't have a home, where to go. And I had to deal-- all of a sudden, someone tells me that if, you know, you don't give me money, I'll, I'll turn your brother in to, to the Slovakian and they'll deport him back over the border. Finally, my brother came and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still living with your uncle at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And after-- at that, at that point in time, my, my, my, my uncle from, my maternal uncle was also deported to a near village near Bratislava. So my brother went to live with them. I lived in Bratislava because I-- a friend of mine got, got me a job in a leather coat factory. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was doing whatever had to be done in a leather factory. I told them that I knew how to sew. But I did not know how to sew. And I told them I only thing I knew how to sew was pants. And they only did leather coats for, for the Slovakian armored vehicle corps, those small jackets and also long coats for the officers made out of leather. And I was going to do all kinds-- they were teaching me how to sew on an electric machine and also whatever had to be done. I cut patterns after someone draw the pattern on the leather. And we just had to, had to cut it out, you know, just to be sure that I set the pattern in such a way that-- where there are no leather deforms or, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a good-paying job, and the, the owner happened to be a Jew who was on false identities. And he had, he also had a, a, a, a rank in the Slovakian army. He was sort of a non-commissioned sergeant, I believe, a master sergeant in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, he yelled at me even though he knew I was Jewish because I didn't do something exactly the way it should have been done. I don't know whether on purpose or whether he just wanted to show the other people that he was strict with me. That's why I lived in Bratislava, and my brother lived with my, with my uncle. Later on, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was, I was very worried. I knew that people were being deported from Hungary. And I also knew that from the community leaders that the, that the Jews were being shipped into, to Auschwitz. And I also knew from the community leaders that two people who escaped from Auschwitz at that time, supposedly they came through Bratislava. And they were helped by the Jewish Underground to smuggle themselves through Austria to Switzerland, where they had some pictures of the gas chambers. And I believed they were the first pictures that the West saw from the gas chambers. And-- but we still, we still hoped that our parents, that my parents and my brothers and sisters were still alive working someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know if they were still-- were there phone lines? Was there no way you could communicate with them in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were phone. I didn't have no telephones in our, in our homes. The only telephones-- while my father had a bank, he had a telephone. After the bank closed, the license was revoked, so he didn't have no phones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you were in the same country, there was no way-- well, of course, actually, you weren't, now. You were back in Slovakia. And they were in now what was Hungary. There was no way for you to know that, that-- whether or not they were safe in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no way. Only rumors. Only from people who came across the border. We knew that, that the people, that the Jewish people from my hometown were all taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you had heard that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had heard that. Then one day, my father showed up, and he told me what happened. He told me how he came. He said that, you know, he was-- there were six transports going out from my hometown. I believe they started in April, and the last one left in June.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of 1944. Since my father was one of the members of the Jewish council, so he was kept till the end. He sort of arranged with their, with a friend of his who, who had a building, who owned a building in the ghetto, that just before they, they will be taken away from the ghetto, that they'll go into hiding with my mother and, and father and my brother and sister. What happened is that the chief of the Gestapo, who was maybe a captain in the SS, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got sick. I believe he needed an appendectomy. And he didn't trust the Hungarian doctors, military doctors, and the military hospital in our town. He wanted to have the, the operation performed in a private hospital through a private doctor. And for that, he would have had to pay money. So he ordered my father to be arrested and to be brought to his bedside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he came to him, he told him he needs 2,000 pengos. That was the Hungarian currency. And he asked him to go out of the ghetto. And there was still some Jewish doctors who were allowed to live outside the ghetto. And some Jewish, baptized Jews who were baptized many, many years ago [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and were allowed to live outside, who were wealthy, and to raise the $2,000, 2,000 pengos and to bring it to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At one point in time, my father and mother, so did my father tell me, they decided that my father should go, even if she'll go into hiding. She will go into hiding, and my father should join us. He was-- they were concerned about our well-being, and we might not survive on our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even though I was, I was 20, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they decided that my father will go across the border to join us and take care of us. And my mother will go into hiding with my father's friends. So after my father was taken in custody, those friends abandoned my mother. And, and they, they were deported. And those people did survive in that hiding place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After my father joined us in-- Let me interrupt you because I'm not clear. What happened with the, with the German soldier, I mean, the officer, the Gestapo, and the, and the money for the operation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father raised the money and gave it to him. And instead of going back into the ghetto, he just went to that person who was smuggling people across the border and asked him to be, to be taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he had allowed him to get out of--

speaker: interviewee
text: He allowed him to get-- although he had a Jewish star, he, the, he had a folder in his, in his hand. And he was covering up his, his star and walked over to that fellow who was smuggling him over and came over the border to join us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after he raised the money and he gave it to the Gestapo officer--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Gestapo officer let him go back out again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he didn't go into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sort of he, he knew-- he felt that there's no other place, there's no place he can go. Only back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, where you had said that these people who abandoned your mother did so when your father was taken into custody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and so that was when the German officer had him arrested--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --so he could raise the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just back off, back up one second. You refer to the ghetto in your, in your hometown. When had that ghetto been instituted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right-- a couple of weeks after the Germans occupied Hungary, they, they ordered-- first they ordered a ghetto to be established. All the Jews living, living outside the perimeter where the ghetto was being formed were ordered to go into the area, move in with other Jews, and, and live there. First we thought this will be a permanent solution or just until the war will be over. But then we didn't know that they had other plans. And they were moving people out of the ghetto into a staging area. It was called the, a brick factory where they had the places like where they were drying bricks before they were kiln burned. And so they had a roof over their head, but no walls. And that was a staging area. That was-- there were railroad tracks close by. So conveniently, they could just herd the people into the cattle vans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, to get a perspective on this in context-- had you still been in your town when the ghetto was formed or had you are already been--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had already-- I, I left already.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were gone, already.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if-- was your home in the area that was part of the ghetto, or did your, did your family have to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family had to move. But we got a good apartment in the ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because of my father's position.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was at that point that your apartment that you had lived in, your family home, was closed. And that's when the man who your father knew protected it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now let's go ahead. You're back in Bratislava. And now your father has joined you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father has joined me. He had some friends. He, he couldn't bring any money out of, out of the ghetto, so he borrowed some more money from some friends whom he knew. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived there for a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when would this have been, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in July, August, and September.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of 1944. The Jewish holidays were early at that time. And my father was praying. He one of, he was one of the, one of the Jews. He stayed in Bratislava. And the factory that I worked in moved to a small town, a small village, near Bratislava. And the reason for that was that we had almost daily raids by the American [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Air Force. And every time there was a raid, the production was interrupted. And in order to get away from these interruptions, they moved the factory to a village next to Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first, I commuted. I lived with my uncle, and I commuted to the small town. Later on, they, they told me it would be much better if I take a room with one of the farmers and, and lived there. I'll be better protected. I wouldn't be in a Jewish environment. And so we took a room, I took a room with a friend of mine and-- with a farmer. Did these people know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- I don't believe they-- they were Hungarians. It was a Hungarian village, but it was in Slovakia. And they didn't know I'm Jewish. They didn't know my friend is Jewish, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your father at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was he living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With relatives. And my brother was with my uncle in another village near Bratislava. I guess people were afraid to have illegal people in their house, the Jews. So they, they told my father, and my uncle told my brother, you know, why don't you move in with your brother? And we felt, maybe, it's even a better idea. You never know when they'll raid the Jewish families. This way, we all had different names, even though my brother resembles me very much, you know. Many people think-- you know, they meet us the first time, they think I'm my brother or my, or vice versa. We still felt we'll take up different identities just in case one of us is caught. And my father also had a different name. My father's name was [NON-ENGLISH]. After--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they move in with the farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They moved in with me. Even though we only had two beds, one for me, one for my friend, they all moved in with us. I slept in one bed with my father. My brother slept with a friend of mine. It wasn't so unusual because they, they knew-- we knew it was only a temporary solution. And there was no other way, you know. What can you do, you know? Put him out? And we couldn't do-- we couldn't get another room under any other pretext. The only pretext that I lived there was because I worked there. That was a defense establishment. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the father know-- I mean, I'm sorry-- the farmer know that you had your brother and father there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was OK with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was OK. We didn't have a problem with that. They were simple people. Good people. Good religious Catholics. And we told them they are from the East Slovakia. They were afraid that the Russians fighting, the fighting will be in their towns, so they came to live with us. And that was sort of a plausible excuse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did everybody speak at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to speak Slovakian because I had a Slovakian name. And so did my brother. And even though they were Hungarian, those people, we tried-- we told them we understand Hungarian but we, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but we don't, we don't speak it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now something came up before. You grew up in-- the town that you were in was part of Eastern Slovakia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were growing up. And yet you said that you weren't familiar with, that familiar with the language. You'd only learned it when you were in school. What did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I, before I started school, we all spoke German because my father's language is German. He grew up in Bratislava, and-- but he spoke Hungarian well, too. But in their house, they spoke German. So my father, my, my paternal parents came from a city in, in Hungary where my great-grandfather, my father's grandfather's grandfather was the rabbi. There was two or three generations of rabbis who came from-- he came from Prague. And at that time, the Jews were persecuted in Prague. Must have been in the 1800s. And when he came to the city of [NON-ENGLISH] in Hungary, he was so grateful that the Hungarians treated the Jews well, that he took the name [NON-ENGLISH.] [NON-ENGLISH] means in German Hungarian.
